title: Concurrent Cache Access, Duplicate Suppression, Ownership, and Race Evidence · Go Retrieval
summary: A delayed ranger review reconstructs concurrent cache access, duplicate suppression, ownership, and race evidence from revision, command, arguments, request identity, response fixture, state transition, cache decision, goroutine ownership, output, failure, cleanup, and recovery evidence. Planned artifact: Extend the evidence record for race-free duplicate-suppressed resource loader after a delayed changed case. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal…
objectives: Protect shared cache invariants with a mutex or owned goroutine and keep lock scope away from slow network work.
Represent one canonical in-flight fetch, waiters, completion, result, and cleanup without leaking entries.
Let each waiter cancel independently while defining when shared underlying work should continue or stop.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

Build deterministic Go evidence to prove duplicate suppression, independent waiters, safe publication, and in-flight cleanup; the fixed PokéAPI origin and fair-use traffic budget must remain enforced; then inject one redirect, control sequence, file, timeout, or persistence failure and reject it safely. Browser Go uses pure bounded models and original fixtures only. Real PokéAPI traffic, DNS, sockets, TLS, host files, terminals, signals, race and leak claims, load, cross-platform packaging, accessibility review, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Use barriers, controlled transports, many interleavings, race runs, duplicate-count assertions, and leak-sensitive cleanup checks.

For Pokedex product engineering and Go Record what required effort · Review Pokedex Go Concurrency Test…, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, failure, race, or toolchain evidence: A normal fast unit-test run can prove absence of data races.
